title: The Starry Night
expected_facts: Painted in June 1889 from the window of his asylum room at Saint-Rémy-de-Provence
Depicts a swirling, turbulent night sky over a village with a prominent cypress tree
Acquired by the Museum of Modern Art in 1941

title: Campbell's Soup Cans
expected_facts: Consists of 32 canvases, one for each of the soup varieties Campbell's sold at the time
A foundational work of American Pop Art
Held by the Museum of Modern Art in New York
license: CC BY-SA (photo of installation); underlying artwork by Andy Warhol

title: Water Lilies
expected_facts: A large three-panel mural-scale depiction of Monet's water-lily pond at Giverny
Part of Monet's late Nymphéas series, painted at Giverny
In the collection of the Museum of Modern Art (accession 712.1959)

title: The Temple of Dendur
maker: Ancient Egypt / Roman Egypt (commissioned by Emperor Augustus)
expected_facts: A Roman-period Egyptian temple built around 15 BC under Emperor Augustus, dedicated to Isis
Originally stood in Nubia; given to the United States by Egypt in 1965 and reassembled at the Met
Housed in the Sackler Wing of the Metropolitan Museum of Art since 1978

title: The Unicorn in Captivity (The Unicorn Rests in a Garden)
medium: wool warp with wool, silk, silver, and gilt wefts (tapestry)
expected_facts: The seventh and final tapestry of the Unicorn Tapestries (Hunt of the Unicorn) series
Depicts a unicorn chained to a pomegranate tree within a low round fence, on a millefleur flowered ground
Woven in the Southern Netherlands around 1495-1505; now at The Met Cloisters

title: Washington Crossing the Delaware
expected_facts: Depicts George Washington leading troops across the ice-filled Delaware River on the night of December 25-26, 1776
A monumental history painting, roughly 12 by 21 feet
In the collection of the Metropolitan Museum of Art

title: The Death of Socrates
expected_facts: A Neoclassical painting depicting Socrates about to drink hemlock while gesturing and discoursing to his grieving disciples
Painted by Jacques-Louis David in 1787
In the collection of the Metropolitan Museum of Art (accession 31.45)

title: The Harvesters
expected_facts: One of a series of six panels depicting the times of the year; this one represents late summer (July-August)
Shows peasants harvesting and resting under a tree in a golden wheat landscape
In the collection of the Metropolitan Museum of Art

title: Autumn Landscape (The River of Life window)
maker: Tiffany Studios (designed by Agnes F. Northrop)
expected_facts: A large leaded-glass landscape window made by Tiffany Studios
Designed by Agnes F. Northrop, one of Tiffany's leading female designers
In the collection of the Metropolitan Museum of Art
license: CC BY-SA (photo); underlying window is public domain

title: Field Armor of King Henry VIII of England
expected_facts: Made for King Henry VIII near the end of his life, constructed for use on horse and on foot
An early example of the flexible overlapping-plate 'anime' breastplate construction
Probably worn at the siege of Boulogne in 1544; now in the Met's Arms and Armor collection

title: Marble statue of a kouros (youth) — the New York Kouros
expected_facts: One of the earliest surviving marble statues of a human figure carved in Attica
Depicts a nude standing youth (kouros) in a rigid frontal pose adapted from Egyptian sculpture
In the Met's Greek and Roman art collection (accession 32.11.1)

title: Hippopotamus ("William")
expected_facts: A small blue faience hippopotamus figurine painted with lotus and marsh plants
Dates to the Egyptian Middle Kingdom (reigns of Senusret I-II); found near Meir
An informal mascot of the Metropolitan Museum of Art, nicknamed 'William'

title: Aquamanile in the Form of Aristotle and Phyllis
expected_facts: A medieval water vessel (aquamanile) used for washing hands, cast in bronze
Depicts the legend of the philosopher Aristotle being ridden by Phyllis
In the Met's medieval art collection

title: Composition 8 (Komposition 8)
expected_facts: A geometric-abstract composition of circles, lines, grids, and angular forms
Painted in 1923 during Kandinsky's Bauhaus period
In the Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum collection
license: Public domain (per Commons; work is faithful reproduction)

title: Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum (building)
expected_facts: Designed by architect Frank Lloyd Wright; completed and opened in 1959
Famous for its white spiral rotunda that widens as it rises, on Fifth Avenue in Manhattan
A landmark of 20th-century modern architecture

title: Early Sunday Morning
expected_facts: Depicts a quiet, sunlit row of storefronts and second-story windows along a deserted street
Painted by Edward Hopper in 1930
In the collection of the Whitney Museum of American Art

title: Macintosh 128K personal computer
maker: Apple Computer (design led by Hartmut Esslinger / frog design; Apple industrial design)
expected_facts: The original Apple Macintosh, introduced in January 1984
An all-in-one beige computer with a built-in 9-inch monochrome CRT and a separate mouse
Represented in the design collection of the Museum of Modern Art
license: Public domain (Bernard Gotfryd photo, Library of Congress)

title: Lounge Chair (model 670) and Ottoman
maker: Charles and Ray Eames (mfr. Herman Miller)
expected_facts: An iconic mid-century modern lounge chair of molded plywood shells and leather cushions
Designed by Charles and Ray Eames in 1956, manufactured by Herman Miller
The design is in the permanent collection of the Museum of Modern Art
license: CC BY-SA (photo of an example of the same design; NGV)

title: Radio-Phonograph (model SK 4/10) — 'Phonosuper', a.k.a. 'Snow White's Coffin'
maker: Dieter Rams and Hans Gugelot (mfr. Braun AG)
medium: painted metal, wood, and acrylic (Plexiglas) lid
expected_facts: A combined valve radio and record player in a closed housing with a clear acrylic lid
Designed by Dieter Rams and Hans Gugelot for Braun in 1956; nicknamed 'Snow White's Coffin'
The design is in the collection of the Museum of Modern Art
license: CC BY-SA (photo of an example of the same design)

title: Walkman TPS-L2 portable cassette player
expected_facts: The first Sony Walkman, a portable personal cassette player released in 1979
Blue and silver body with two headphone jacks; pioneered personal portable music
The design is represented in the Museum of Modern Art collection
license: CC BY-SA (photo of an example of the same design)

title: The Great Canoe (Northwest Coast dugout canoe)
medium: carved and painted western red cedar
expected_facts: A 63-foot sea-going dugout canoe carved from a single western red cedar log
Made by Northwest Coast Haida and Heiltsuk artists; acquired by the museum around 1883
Displayed in the Northwest Coast Hall of the American Museum of Natural History

title: Northwest Coast totem poles
maker: Northwest Coast Indigenous artists (Haida and neighboring nations)
expected_facts: Carved and painted cedar totem poles from Northwest Coast Indigenous cultures
Displayed in the Northwest Coast Hall of the American Museum of Natural History
The hall's collection includes dozens of poles alongside the Great Canoe

title: Lockheed A-12 (Blackbird), often labeled SR-71
expected_facts: A Lockheed A-12 (the single-seat CIA reconnaissance predecessor of the SR-71 Blackbird)
Built by Lockheed's Skunk Works; a Mach 3+ titanium spy plane
Displayed at the Intrepid Sea, Air & Space Museum in New York (aircraft 60-6925)

title: Space Shuttle Enterprise (OV-101)
expected_facts: The first Space Shuttle orbiter, a prototype used for atmospheric approach-and-landing tests (never flew to space)
Delivered to the Intrepid Museum and displayed in its Space Shuttle Pavilion from 2012
Built for NASA by Rockwell International; designated OV-101

title: Concorde G-BOAD
expected_facts: A British Airways Concorde supersonic passenger jet (registration G-BOAD)
Holds the record for the fastest Atlantic crossing by a Concorde
On display at the Intrepid Sea, Air & Space Museum in New York
